video_title: Why Trend Following Is Harder Than It Looks | Systematic Investor | Ep.405
gist: Rob Carver and Niels Kaastrup-Larsen dissect trend-following construction problems: degraded US economic data hurting macro strategies, the new CFTC-approved crypto perpetual futures and the CME lawsuit against it, Quantica's finding that single-factor regimes (not diversification) drive trend returns, and overfitting risk in the AI back-testing era.

themes.theme: Degraded US economic data as a macro-strategy risk

insights.claim: New Fed Chair Kevin Walsh signaled a more hawkish tone in his first FOMC meeting, plans to exit forward guidance, and wants to rework the data the central bank releases including scrapping some older survey methods.
insights.speaker: Niels Kaastrup-Larsen
insights.so_what: Removal of forward guidance and survey data raises volatility in rate expectations and could disrupt the data inputs that economic-trend and systematic-macro strategies depend on.
insights.quote: forward guidance is not a business that the Fed should be in according to him.

insights.claim: Systematic global-macro and economic-trend strategies rely on government statistical data, so deliberate Fed/government degradation of that data is a direct operational risk to those strategies, while pure price-based trend followers are unaffected.
insights.speaker: Niels Kaastrup-Larsen
insights.so_what: Investors allocating to economic-trend or macro sleeves should question data dependence; pure price-based CTAs offer insulation from data-quality deterioration.
insights.quote: for the pure price-based trend followers, um, we don't care.

insights.claim: US statistical data quality has already degraded materially due to DOGE spending cuts and last year's government shutdown, which delayed or prevented data collection.
insights.speaker: Rob Carver
insights.so_what: Lower-quality official data makes price discovery and economic forecasting harder, creating an edge for third-party data providers that reconstruct official figures.
insights.quote: there's already been quite a serious degradation of the quality of statistical data in the US, partly as a result of um the the Doge cuts.

insights.claim: Niels's trend barometer finished at 36, signaling a weak trend environment, with headwinds in equities, metals (aluminum) and grains, while short rate positions and currencies (long dollar) have been working after the hawkish Fed.
insights.speaker: Niels Kaastrup-Larsen
insights.so_what: A barometer of 36 with low realized risk signals muted trend opportunity; CTAs sitting short rates and long dollar are the current consensus positioning to watch.
insights.quote: My trend barometer finished at 36 yesterday. So, it's weak uh for sure.

insights.claim: The biggest market move in June 2026 was a roughly 15% drop in the oil complex, with silver and platinum each down about 9%, offset by equities such as the Nikkei futures up about 7% on the month.
insights.speaker: Niels Kaastrup-Larsen
insights.so_what: Commodity weakness against equity strength defines June's dispersion; the oil drop reflects the Middle East peace deal that markets had largely pre-priced.
insights.quote: the big market move so far in June has really been in the oil complex where we've seen kind of 15% uh drops in price

insights.claim: Carver runs only about 9% annualized risk currently with his largest position a long equity exposure and flat in energies, reflecting the weak trend regime.
insights.speaker: Rob Carver
insights.so_what: A systematic trader voluntarily cutting to ~9% risk confirms that trend signals are weak and that capital should not be force-deployed in low-conviction regimes.
insights.quote: 9 9% analyzeed risk, which kind of equates to your your trend barometer numbers

insights.claim: The Middle East oil-price reaction looks like a 'buy the rumor, sell the fact' event because markets had already priced the optimistic peace-deal scenario before it was announced.
insights.speaker: Rob Carver
insights.so_what: Further downside in oil may be limited and supply-shortage knock-on risks remain, so the deal is not a clean bullish or bearish catalyst.
insights.quote: maybe this is one of those classic cases of, you know, buy the rumor, sell the sell the fact

insights.claim: Carver argues drawdown depth and duration carry little information for evaluating a strategy because drawdowns scale mechanically with track-record length and inversely with the Sharpe ratio.
insights.speaker: Rob Carver
insights.so_what: Allocators over-weighting drawdown statistics risk penalizing good long-track-record managers; Sharpe is the more honest core metric.
insights.quote: the more history you have, the bigger your draw down will be, the better a strategy is, the higher it sharp ratio, the smaller the draw down will be.

insights.claim: Carver uses skew as a classification tool to bucket strategies into convergent versus divergent, capping negative-skew strategies (e.g. at 30%) rather than blending skew and Sharpe into a single utility function.
insights.speaker: Rob Carver
insights.so_what: Treating skew as a classifier rather than an optimizer input avoids overfitting to noisy skew estimates while still controlling tail-risk exposure.
insights.quote: I want to limit say 30% of my portfolio to negative skew strategies and no more therefore I'm going to just categorize things and then do my allocation within that side.

insights.claim: Skew is not obvious by strategy type: carry in emerging-market FX has negative skew while carry in developed-market government bonds has mildly positive skew.
insights.speaker: Rob Carver
insights.so_what: Lumping all carry as a single negative-skew sleeve mis-measures tail risk; the asset class determines whether carry diversifies or concentrates tail exposure.
insights.quote: carry in in emerging markets FX definitely has negative skew carry in in you know um developed government bonds has actually mildly positive skew

insights.claim: Virtually every CTA blends 20-30% non-trend (convergent) strategies; Carver runs a higher ~40% because he has no end-client allocator box to fit into.
insights.speaker: Rob Carver
insights.so_what: Allocators should recognize most 'trend' products are blends, and that client-driven constraints, not pure performance, often dictate the trend/non-trend mix.
insights.quote: my own allocation to those is a 40% which is higher than CTAs because I don't have a end client pool to worry about.

insights.claim: Carver says he rarely drops a strategy and would only do so given very strong statistical evidence of a structural break that it has stopped working.
insights.speaker: Rob Carver
insights.so_what: A high bar for removing strategies guards against acting on noise; investors should demand statistical evidence of a break, not just recent underperformance.
insights.quote: I rarely drop a strategy as I'd only do so if if there was very strong statistical evidence that it stopped working.

themes.theme: Trend-following as equity beta plus alpha

insights.claim: Because equities tend to rise, a trend follower on equities is on average net long, so a large part of returns is simply the equity risk premium with trend adding extra return when short during down moves.
insights.speaker: Rob Carver
insights.so_what: Investors should decompose trend returns into embedded equity beta versus genuine timing alpha when sizing trend against an existing equity book.
insights.quote: if you say trend follow equities, you will be on average net long equities because equities tend to go up.

insights.claim: If a trend sleeve is only ~5-20% of a 60/40 portfolio, theory favors a more market-neutral trend design (only long when markets rise a lot) to avoid doubling the equity beta already held elsewhere.
insights.speaker: Rob Carver
insights.so_what: Small-sleeve allocators can improve total-portfolio efficiency by lowering trend's average beta, though increasing the trend allocation outright is the cleaner fix.
insights.quote: rather than being long the market when it's going up, you'd probably be only long the market when it's going up a lot.

insights.claim: Kaastrup-Larsen disagrees with adjusting a trend program to fit other holdings, arguing that if you have too much equity you should sell the equity and let trend run, since artificially reducing sectors distorts inter-market interactions.
insights.speaker: Niels Kaastrup-Larsen
insights.so_what: A counterpoint to portfolio-tilting: hollowing out a trend program's sectors can break its internal correlation structure and forfeit short exposure in crises.
insights.quote: I think if you feel you have too much equity, you sell the equity. You let the trend following program run

insights.claim: A key value of trend following is exposure to markets investors cannot easily access or find too risky themselves, especially commodities, plus the ability to go short in prolonged crises.
insights.speaker: Niels Kaastrup-Larsen
insights.so_what: The diversification case for trend rests on hard-to-reach markets and unbiased long/short capability, arguing for commodity-heavy rather than financial-heavy tilts.
insights.quote: it adds the markets you don't trade yourself

themes.theme: Perpetual futures and the CME lawsuit

insights.claim: The CFTC approved the first-ever perpetual futures contract in the US, linked to crypto, which unlike all prior futures has no expiration date and effectively settles daily.
insights.speaker: Niels Kaastrup-Larsen
insights.so_what: A new contract structure that eliminates rolls and keeps futures pinned to spot could reshape how systematic traders access markets and manage roll mechanics.
insights.quote: they approved um what uh we could refer to as a perpetual futures contract... has no expiration date.

insights.claim: Perpetual futures are economically equivalent to daily-settling futures whose price must equal spot under a no-arbitrage argument, making interest-rate and carry components negligible enough to ignore.
insights.speaker: Rob Carver
insights.so_what: Traders modeling perpetuals can largely drop carry/storage/dividend basis adjustments, simplifying signal construction but compressing market-maker basis edges.
insights.quote: every time the bet settles, which could be daily or could even be more frequently than daily, the prices must be identical

insights.claim: CME Group sued the CFTC over the perpetual-futures approval, arguing the contract should be classified as a swap, because perpetual futures eliminate rolls and would significantly reduce exchange trading volume.
insights.speaker: Niels Kaastrup-Larsen
insights.so_what: A legal fight over contract classification signals an existential threat to roll-driven exchange volumes and a potential migration of liquidity toward OTC swaps or market-maker platforms.
insights.quote: they have uh essentially as I picked up this morning uh yesterday sued the CFTC over this decision to approve a perpetual futures in the US.

insights.claim: Carver warns perpetual futures undermine the original hedging purpose of futures, since a farmer needs a dated contract matching harvest timing, and migration to OTC swaps would split liquidity and widen spreads.
insights.speaker: Rob Carver
insights.so_what: Loss of dated contracts could damage real price discovery and hedging in commodities, a structural negative for the futures ecosystem despite trader convenience.
insights.quote: If you're a farmer hedging your crop, well, you absolutely want a dated future.

themes.theme: Quantica regime study: single-factor drivers vs diversification

insights.claim: Quantica's 'Chasing Trends or Chasing Performance' paper shows trend returns rotated by asset class: broad-based (equities/currencies/commodities) in 2000-2009, all fixed income in 2010-2019, and almost entirely commodities in 2020-2026.
insights.speaker: Rob Carver
insights.so_what: Trend performance is regime-dependent by asset class, so a portfolio built only on the last decade's winners (e.g. fixed income) would have whipsawed into the commodity era.
insights.quote: if we look at the last seven years, that's co COVID onwards, 2020 to 2026, it's all commodities pretty much.

insights.claim: Quantica finds that strong trend performance in a dominant asset class comes from a single common factor moving everything together, not from diversification, meaning correlation actually falls but everything moves up at once.
insights.speaker: Rob Carver
insights.so_what: Best-performing trend regimes reward concentration into a dominant factor, which is uncomfortable for diversification-maximizing allocators expecting broad-based gains.
insights.quote: in these periods when one asset class is dominating it's very much about a a sort of single factor that's driving everything. It's not a diversification story.

insights.claim: Quantica concludes that a continuous (non-binary) trend system that scales positions by trend strength already tilts the portfolio toward the strongest-trending asset classes, so explicitly chasing recent winners adds nothing.
insights.speaker: Rob Carver
insights.so_what: Managers running continuous signals get the regime tilt for free; bolting on performance-chasing overlays risks overfitting without added return.
insights.quote: by allocating to things with stronger trends which is what you do naturally if you've got a continuous system that allocation is already happening.

insights.claim: Kaastrup-Larsen argues the 2010-2019 fixed-income-only decade was the anomaly driven by central banks holding rates near zero, and that the 1970s-80s-90s and 2020s resemble each other with strong commodity contribution.
insights.speaker: Niels Kaastrup-Larsen
insights.so_what: If the zero-rate decade was the outlier, the structurally normal trend regime is commodity-rich, reinforcing the case for commodity-heavy diversified portfolios.
insights.quote: that decade was not the new norm. It's the odd one out.

insights.claim: Kaastrup-Larsen cites a reputable-firm study finding the commodity sector is the most consistent trend-following contributor during crisis periods, contrasting with the industry drift toward liquid financial markets.
insights.speaker: Niels Kaastrup-Larsen
insights.so_what: Commodity exposure is the most reliable crisis diversifier, so financial-heavy CTA portfolios driven by capacity and fee incentives may sacrifice crisis robustness.
insights.quote: the most consistent sector in a crisis is the commodity sector.

themes.theme: Overfitting in the AI back-testing era

insights.claim: Carver estimates about 40% of his LinkedIn/Twitter feed is AI-generated strategy back tests, and warns AI now lets practitioners overfit automatically at scale.
insights.speaker: Rob Carver
insights.so_what: A flood of AI-generated back tests raises the base rate of overfit strategies, demanding more skepticism of any unvetted quantitative track record.
insights.quote: whenever I go on LinkedIn or even Twitter, about 40% of my feed is people with producing back tests of strategies that have obviously been generated by by AI

insights.claim: Carver argues AI is acceptable for generating strategy ideas but the robust out-of-sample testing process matters far more than the math, and AI-generated ideas must be tested away from the AI.
insights.speaker: Rob Carver
insights.so_what: Due diligence should focus on the research process from idea to live trading regardless of whether AI generated the idea, since the source matters less than the validation rigor.
insights.quote: it's fine to use AI essentially to generate ideas... the most important thing is the process that you follow.

insights.claim: Carver says a back test should output a probability that an idea works, which maps to a portfolio weight, rather than a binary yes/no, so even weak ideas keep tiny allocations like 0.01%.
insights.speaker: Rob Carver
insights.so_what: Treating strategy inclusion as a probabilistic weighting avoids hard cuts that discard low-probability winners like cocoa, improving long-run robustness.
insights.quote: it's not really a yes or no question... It's going to be a percentage. It's going to be a probability that it's good and that will translate then into a portfolio weight

insights.claim: Carver warns that strategies built on public AI models are especially dangerous because there is no control over whether the training data is real or contains forward-looking information.
insights.speaker: Rob Carver
insights.so_what: Internet-marketed AI trading products likely rely on public models with unknowable look-ahead bias, making them a high-risk place to deploy capital.
insights.quote: if it's a public AI model then you all bets are off you know you've no idea what you know where the data is coming from whether the data is real
